questions.question:
Which of the following is/are correct?
A. An SQL query automatically eliminates duplicates
B. An SQL query will not work if there are no indexes on the relations
C. SQL permits attribute names to be repeated in the same relation
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) about SQL behaviour are correct

understand.plain: This question tests basic knowledge of how SQL handles duplicates, indexes, and attribute naming. SQL does NOT automatically remove duplicates (you must use DISTINCT). SQL works fine without indexes (indexes only affect performance). SQL does NOT allow duplicate attribute names in the same relation/table.

keywords.explain: A keyword in SQL SELECT that removes duplicate rows from the result set; without it, duplicates are retained.
keywords.example: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM employees; -- removes duplicate city names

keywords.explain: A data structure that speeds up retrieval of rows from a table; queries can execute correctly without indexes, just potentially slower.
keywords.example: CREATE INDEX idx_emp ON employees(emp_no); -- speeds up lookups but not required for correctness

keywords.explain: The definition of a table including its attribute (column) names, which must be unique within a relation.
keywords.example: EMP(emp_no, name, salary) -- all three attribute names are distinct

understand.visual_alt: Three SQL properties evaluated: duplicate handling (SELECT vs SELECT DISTINCT), index requirement (optional), attribute name uniqueness (required).

given.aim: Identify which statement about SQL is true

terms.meaning: Claim that a plain SELECT removes duplicate rows automatically
terms.example: SELECT city FROM emp -- does NOT remove duplicates by default
terms.connects: FALSE -- SQL SELECT retains duplicates; DISTINCT is needed

terms.term: Statement B: SQL needs indexes to work
terms.meaning: Claim that SQL queries fail if no indexes exist on the tables
terms.example: A full table scan is performed when no index exists -- query still returns correct results
terms.connects: FALSE -- indexes are optional optimisations, not requirements for correctness

terms.term: Statement C: SQL allows duplicate attribute names in a relation
terms.meaning: Claim that a table can have two columns with the same name
terms.example: CREATE TABLE t(a INT, a INT) -- this is illegal in SQL
terms.connects: FALSE -- attribute names within a relation must be unique

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against fundamental SQL semantics. All three are incorrect, so the answer is D.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.apply: In SQL, a plain SELECT returns all rows including duplicates. Only SELECT DISTINCT eliminates duplicates. So Statement A is FALSE.
steps.note: This is a common misconception; SQL is a multiset (bag) model by default, not a set model.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.apply: SQL queries execute correctly without any indexes; the DBMS performs a full table scan. Indexes only improve performance. So Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: Indexes are a physical design decision; they are transparent to query correctness.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C
steps.apply: SQL requires all attribute names within a single relation to be unique. Duplicate attribute names would make it impossible to reference a specific column. So Statement C is FALSE.
steps.note: This is enforced at the schema level; two different relations can share attribute names, but not the same relation.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Since A, B, and C are all false, the correct answer is D: None of the above.
steps.note: All three statements contain well-known SQL misconceptions tested in GATE.

questions.formula_note: Basic SQL semantics: SELECT (multiset semantics), index-independence of query correctness, uniqueness of attribute names within a relation.

questions.question:
Consider the set of relations:
  EMP (Employee-no, Dept-no, Employee-name, Salary)
  DEPT (Dept-no, Dept-name, Location)

Write an SQL query to:
a. Find all employee names who work in departments located at 'Calcutta' and whose salary is greater than Rs. 50,000.
b. Calculate, for each department number, the number of employees with a salary greater than Rs. 1,00,000.
questions.answer:
a. SELECT E.Employee-name FROM EMP E, DEPT D WHERE E.Dept-no = D.Dept-no AND D.Location = 'Calcutta' AND E.Salary > 50000;
b. SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;
questions.to_find: SQL queries to (a) find employee names in Calcutta with salary > 50000, and (b) count employees per department with salary > 100000

understand.plain: This is a write-the-query question using two related tables: EMP (employees) and DEPT (departments). Part (a) requires a join with a filter on location and salary. Part (b) requires GROUP BY with COUNT and a salary filter.

keywords.explain: Combining rows from two tables based on a matching column value; here EMP and DEPT share Dept-no.
keywords.example: FROM EMP E, DEPT D WHERE E.Dept-no = D.Dept-no

keywords.explain: Aggregates rows that share the same value in specified columns, enabling aggregate functions like COUNT per group.
keywords.example: GROUP BY Dept-no -- gives one row per department

keywords.explain: An aggregate function that counts the number of rows in each group.
keywords.example: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP GROUP BY Dept-no -- counts employees per dept

understand.visual_alt: EMP table joined with DEPT table on Dept-no; part (a) filters on Location='Calcutta' and Salary>50000; part (b) groups EMP by Dept-no and counts rows where Salary>100000.

given.aim: Write SQL queries for filtering with a join and for grouped aggregation

terms.meaning: Employee table with department reference and salary
terms.connects: Source of employee name and salary data; joined with DEPT for location
Provides Location to filter departments by city in part (a)

terms.meaning: Threshold filters applied to the Salary column in WHERE clause
terms.connects: Restricts rows before projection in part (a); filters before aggregation in part (b)

given.plan: Part (a): join EMP and DEPT on Dept-no, filter Location='Calcutta' and Salary>50000, project Employee-name. Part (b): filter EMP where Salary>100000, then GROUP BY Dept-no with COUNT(*).

steps.title: Part (a) — Employee names in Calcutta earning > 50,000
steps.formula_raw: SELECT E.Employee-name FROM EMP E, DEPT D WHERE E.Dept-no = D.Dept-no AND D.Location = 'Calcutta' AND E.Salary > 50000;
steps.apply: Join EMP (alias E) and DEPT (alias D) on the common key Dept-no. Add conditions Location = 'Calcutta' and Salary > 50000 in WHERE clause. Project Employee-name.
steps.note: The join condition E.Dept-no = D.Dept-no is essential to link each employee to their department's location.

steps.title: Part (b) — Count of high-earners per department
steps.formula_raw: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;
steps.apply: Filter EMP for rows where Salary > 100000 (i.e., > Rs. 1,00,000). Then GROUP BY Dept-no and use COUNT(*) to get the number of such employees in each department.
steps.note: No join with DEPT is needed here since only the department number (not name/location) is required in the output.

solution.result:
a. SELECT E.Employee-name FROM EMP E, DEPT D WHERE E.Dept-no = D.Dept-no AND D.Location = 'Calcutta' AND E.Salary > 50000;
b. SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;

questions.formula_note: SQL join with equi-join condition in WHERE clause; GROUP BY with COUNT aggregate for per-department statistics.

questions.question:
Consider the set of relations:
  EMP (Employee-no, Dept-no, Employee-name, Salary)
  DEPT (Dept-no, Dept-name, Location)

Write an SQL query to:
Calculate, for each department number, the number of employees with a salary greater than Rs. 1,00,000.
questions.answer: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;
questions.to_find: SQL query to count, per department, employees earning more than Rs. 1,00,000

understand.plain: This question asks for a grouped aggregation query. We need to look inside the EMP table, keep only rows where the salary exceeds Rs. 1,00,000, then group the remaining rows by department number and count how many employees are in each group.

keywords.explain: Partitions result rows into groups based on a column value; aggregate functions operate on each group independently.
keywords.example: GROUP BY Dept-no -- one output row per distinct Dept-no

keywords.explain: Counts the total number of rows in each group, including all non-null rows.
keywords.example: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP GROUP BY Dept-no -- gives headcount per dept

keywords.explain: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation.
keywords.example: WHERE Salary > 100000 -- filters rows before COUNT is applied

understand.visual_alt: EMP table rows filtered by Salary > 100000, then grouped by Dept-no; COUNT(*) computed for each group.

given.aim: Count employees per department where salary > Rs. 1,00,000

terms.meaning: Employee table containing salary and department information
terms.connects: The table we query; Dept-no is the grouping key, Salary is the filter attribute

terms.meaning: Filter condition selecting only high-earning employees (salary > Rs. 1,00,000)
terms.connects: Applied in WHERE clause before GROUP BY to restrict the rows counted

terms.meaning: The attribute by which results are aggregated — one count per distinct department number
terms.connects: Determines the granularity of the output; each department gets its own count

given.plan: Filter EMP rows to Salary > 100000, then apply GROUP BY Dept-no with COUNT(*) to get the per-department count.

steps.title: Filter rows by salary threshold
steps.apply: Add a WHERE clause to restrict the EMP table to only those employees whose salary is greater than Rs. 1,00,000 (100000). This happens before grouping.
steps.note: Using WHERE (not HAVING) is correct here because we filter individual rows, not aggregate groups.

steps.title: Group by department and count
steps.formula_raw: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;
steps.apply: After filtering, partition the remaining rows by Dept-no using GROUP BY. COUNT(*) then counts the number of qualifying employees in each department. The result has one row per department that has at least one employee with Salary > 100000.
steps.note: Departments with no employees earning above 100000 will not appear in the output (no row generated for zero-count groups unless OUTER JOIN or COALESCE is used).

solution.result: SELECT Dept-no, COUNT(*) FROM EMP WHERE Salary > 100000 GROUP BY Dept-no;

questions.formula_note: SQL GROUP BY with COUNT aggregate; WHERE clause filters rows before aggregation (pre-aggregation filter).

questions.question:
Given relations r(w, x) and s(y, z), the result of

  SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s

is guaranteed to be the same as r, provided:
A. r has no duplicates and s is non-empty
B. r and s have no duplicates
C. s has no duplicates and r is non-empty
D. r and s have the same number of tuples
questions.answer: A. r has no duplicates and s is non-empty
questions.to_find: Condition under which SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s equals r

understand.plain: The query computes the Cartesian product of r and s, projects only columns (w, x) from r, and removes duplicates. For the result to equal r exactly, every tuple of r must appear and no extra tuples should appear. Since projection on (w,x) after cross-product always includes all (w,x) pairs from r (as long as s is non-empty), and DISTINCT removes repeats, the result equals r precisely when r itself has no duplicates and s is non-empty.

keywords.explain: Combining every row of one table with every row of another, producing |r| x |s| rows.
keywords.example: r has 3 rows, s has 2 rows => cross product has 6 rows

keywords.explain: Returns unique rows from the result set, eliminating duplicate tuples.
keywords.example: SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s -- removes repeated (w,x) pairs

keywords.explain: Selecting only specified columns from a relation, which can introduce duplicates if projected columns do not form a key.
keywords.example: Projecting (w,x) from the cross product retains original r values but repeats each |s| times

understand.visual_alt: r(w,x) cross-joined with s(y,z) produces |r|x|s| rows; projecting (w,x) and applying DISTINCT gives back r's distinct tuples. If r has no duplicates and s is non-empty, the result equals r exactly.

given.aim: Find the condition that makes SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s return exactly the relation r

terms.meaning: A relation with attributes w and x; the target relation we want the query to reproduce
terms.connects: Columns (w,x) are the ones projected in SELECT; we need the result to match r exactly

terms.meaning: A second relation used only to form the Cartesian product; contributes no columns to the output
terms.connects: s must be non-empty so the cross product includes every row of r at least once

terms.meaning: After Cartesian product, each (w,x) row from r appears |s| times; DISTINCT collapses these back to one copy
terms.example: If r has 2 rows and s has 3 rows, projecting w,x gives 6 rows; DISTINCT gives 2 (if r has no dups)
terms.connects: DISTINCT undoes the cross-product repetitions, leaving exactly the distinct (w,x) pairs; if r itself had duplicates, DISTINCT would reduce them too, making the result a proper subset of r

given.plan: Trace what the query returns: cross product gives every (w,x,y,z) combo; projecting (w,x) gives each r-row repeated |s| times; DISTINCT removes repetitions. Result = set of distinct (w,x) tuples in r. This equals r iff (1) r has no duplicate (w,x) tuples (otherwise DISTINCT reduces count) and (2) s is non-empty (otherwise cross product is empty).

steps.title: Understand the query semantics
steps.formula_raw: result = pi_{w,x}(r x s) with duplicates removed
steps.apply: FROM r, s computes the Cartesian product r x s. SELECT w, x projects onto r's attributes. DISTINCT removes duplicate (w,x) rows. So the result is the set of distinct (w,x) values appearing in r (since y,z from s contribute nothing to the projection).
steps.note: The result is independent of the actual content of s; s only affects whether the cross product is empty or not.

steps.title: Condition for result = r
steps.apply:
The result equals r iff:
(i) s is non-empty: if s is empty, r x s is empty, so the result is empty, not r.
(ii) r has no duplicate (w,x) tuples: if r has duplicates, DISTINCT collapses them, producing fewer rows than r.
Both conditions together: r has no duplicates AND s is non-empty.
steps.note: Whether s has duplicates is irrelevant — projecting away y,z makes all s-rows indistinguishable, and DISTINCT on (w,x) does not depend on s content.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: Option A states: 'r has no duplicates and s is non-empty' -- this exactly matches our derived condition. Options B, C, D do not correctly capture both requirements (C wrongly focuses on s having no duplicates, which is irrelevant).
steps.note: Option C is the classic distractor: it requires s to have no duplicates, but that is irrelevant since s columns are projected away.

solution.result: A. r has no duplicates and s is non-empty

questions.formula_note: Relational algebra: Cartesian product followed by projection and DISTINCT; result equivalence requires the projected relation to be duplicate-free and the other relation to be non-empty.
